title: 2025/11/06 - ENV
description: Thursday, 6th November 2025 | Environment Committee Briefing on BioInvest Hearing of evidence: Department of Conservation Stephanie Rowe, Deputy Director-General Biodiversity Tim Bamford, Director Terrestrial Biodiversity Richard Maloney, Principal Adviser Biodiversity
descriptionSummary: Briefing on BioInvest. Department of Conservation. Stephanie Rowe, Deputy Director-General Biodiversity

chunks.text: Okay. Oh well good morning. Thank you so much for having us. We're really happy to be here to talk about something we're really proud of that we've done. So, I'm Stephanie Rip. You've met me before. I'm one of our Deputy Director generals and I just want to introduce Richard Maloney, who's one of our principal scientists, and Tim Bamford, who's our director of Terrestrial Biodiversity. So you comfortable, we thought we might talk for about 15 minutes and then leave space for questions. Does that Yes. Perfect. That'd be great.

Thank you. Alright, so I'm not, I'm not gonna say too much. I'm gonna leave it to the experts to talk about this stuff. But just to recap, um, so we've talked about this a few times before, so if we just talk to the next slide. So, um, one of the things that we wanted to build was, uh, basically to develop the recipe of what would it take to get all of the species and ecosystems to be thriving.

Um, and so that's the work that the team have done. So really over many decades and longer, there's been a whole lot of knowledge of what it takes, the, the tools, the research, the skills, and we've looked across everything today. What would, what would it look like if you did all of that? And therefore how do we make the best choices of what we, um, can afford to do today?

And if we got more investment, what more would we do? And it helps you pick the next best thing that you might work on depending on, um, the criteria that you might have. And it helped us think about the fact, if you look across, we were quite good at going, here's what we need to do in French water and marine and predators. We were good at prioritizing within the line, but it's how do you actually look across all of that to get the balance across everything?

'cause you can't just do one thing. Mm-hmm. You've gotta make sure you get a good balance. So we could get to predator Free 2050, which would be phenomenal, but that's not gonna help the marine environment necessarily, or the freshwater environment. So we've gotta get that, that balance across. And so the team will take you into the tool. It's an interactive tool, but we've just done screenshots 'cause it's just easier than, um, trying to play with technology.

Yeah. So, um, yeah, so instead of having that issue by issue investment, we are trying to make sure that we've got that prioritized investment across everything we are doing. That we are really clear that we've got good return on the investment. We know the impact we're having in terms of the outcomes we're trying to achieve for biodiversity. That it's really transparent and clear why we are investing in different things.

Um, and we understand the trade trade-offs that we are making across the different work that we are doing. Um, and in the past we've often talked about how many hectares of an activity that we do that's important. We wanna know how much of an activity, but just because you've done x hectares of weed control doesn't mean that you've reached the right biodiversity outcome that you might wanna reach.

So we are making sure we've built a system that for each activity we know what outcome it actually leads to and what we're actually doing to save the things that we wanna save. So that's just a bit of a recap, but I'm gonna hand to the team, um, to get into the detail. Right. So, um, where we started was think like all of our work, as Steph said, has predominantly been within a particular area like birds, or was it for example.

So where we started was thinking about how do we group up our biodiversity work so we can think across Lt Aurora, what are we achieving in terms of the species of the ecosystems? And then also cut that spatially and understand in new Plymouth, what are we achieving, what are we achieving, um, on, for example. And so we created a program structure for biodiversity to help us jump up and down those different levels in decision making.

And so we took like an interventionist approach. So the basic level and at really granular, we look after species at a really kind of narrow level. And then we pull up and we look after ecosystems and then we pull back even more and we manage threats across a larger landscape area. Um, and then also we have a bunch of questions like, for example, a Kiwi population may be declining and we dunno why that is.

And so we need to answer why is that declining? So we need to undertake research to understand particular questions. And then often once we know something is declining or something is happening, we may not have the tools, um, to manage or improve that particular thing. Um, so for example, um, before we developed aerial control for, um, for rats and possums for example.

And so we need to undertake some research to develop tools. So we thought the, the main four groups of delivery work we do is looking after species, looking after ecosystems, looking after threats, and undertaking a bunch of research. So we created those four program groups you see. And then in addition, um, as a department we have a bunch of obligations like our rams are sites, obligations with councils and some treaty settlements and other obligations that cut across all of, um, those four areas.

But we do them because they're obligations predominantly. Um, so we pulled that out as a separate program group. Um, and then we have a support program group. 'cause we have things like providing guidance to data, et cetera that we need to do in the background to be clear that we're doing as a department, but kind of sits outside of those areas.

Um, and so we created those six program groups and we looked at all of the biodiversity across al row and created the 33 programs that you can see within them that defines all of the biodiversity work, um, that we need and would like to do. Um, and you can go to the next slide. Um, and so then what we've done is for each program we've created a clear narrative which explains what's the objective of looking after threatened species, what's in and out of scope, what's the criteria for undertaking that, um, for prioritizing those activities.

So for example, um, in the threatened species program, there's four and a half thousand threatened species. You generally wanna prioritize the ones that are closest to, um, being irreversibly lost. Um, but you also want to think about things about what is the difference in looking into the undertaking those actions to look after that threatened species compared to doing nothing.

But also what's the probability of, um, being successful and are you duplicating it? Are you already looking after those species or ecosystems? So what does, um, choosing to do something, how does that add to the overarching picture? Um, whether there are treaty settlements that create commitments to those particular species, et cetera.

So all of that sits in the background in terms of, um, these criteria. Um, and then we've created, you can go to the, um, next slide to one more please. Um, we've created individual outcome plans, so basically recipes for each species, um, that sit in the background that help us understand if we do want to, um, look after the southern on rocky aura, what do we need to do?

So it's saying there are three main sites, two on rocky aura, um, that you really need to be managing the impacts of, um, presentation, um, on the, the birds, um, and the eggs. And then also, um, on the mainland of the south island, um, there's some recreation, like four wheel driving that has impacts, um, on the birds.

So you need to be managing that too, so you can quickly cost it up and understand if we do those actions, you'll be looking after, um, looking after the southern doctoral. And so a few jump back aside, um, we have over 14,000 of those outcome plans sitting within, um, those 33 programs.

So there's a heap of choices. That's a lot. Um, going back to Steph's point before, like this is really about informing decisions. So what's information is enabling us to do is have really transparent conversations about what do we want to achieve.

It's unrealistic where even gonna do the budget that we need, we can't do everything. So we need to be really informed and clear about what, and there are why we want to choose to do. But this is now enabling us to inform discussions and also provide advice through really understanding like what are the outcomes that we wanna achieve for a particular place, for a particular species or ecosystem and for Aldi.

Um, and understand what are the costs of that and what does a different set of options enable you to do. So if you, um, now go two slides along, please. Um, so we're just gonna run you through a few of the tools that we've, um, created based on this information.

Um, that's enabling us, um, to have these conversations. And so, um, we've put all of these outcome plans, they go onto the computer and then we, um, visualize them. So you can now look across, um, a range of different aspects and kind of understand what are our choices.
